coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots based MnFe2O4@SiO2 magnetic nanostructure as a pH-sensitive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system to enhance the anticancer effect of the drug
coredata.dc:creator: Kiani Nejad, Zahra
Akbar Khandar, Ali
Khatamian, Massoumeh
coredata.dc:description: Among the various methods of targeted drug delivery, magnetic nanoparticles been considered for a long time due to local drug delivery, reduction of side effects, and controlled drug release. In this work, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU with 28.02 emu g-1 magnetism was prepared as pH-sensitive nanoplatform to enhance the anti-cancer effect of daunorubicin (DAU) drug (in the obtained FRET system, DAU act as acceptor molecule and graphene quantum dots act as donor molecule). The efficiency of the drug loaded on the nanoplatform in vitro is 78 %. DAU drug release from nanoplatform at pHs of 7.4 and 5.5 during 48 h is 21 % and 60 %, respectively. Release sensitive to pH facilitates the application of prepared nanoplatform for DAU delivery. The results of MTT-assay and annexin V-FITC/PI show that MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU induces cell apoptosis by inhibiting the growth of more than 95 % of MCF-7 cells. Also, according to the results, it was found that MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU can inhibit 66.65 % cell cycle in the sub-G1 phase. Therefore, due to the anti-cancer activity of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU, this biological nanoscale can be considered a candidate for drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cell cycle
Daunorubicin (DAU)
Apoptosis
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles coated with a silica layer
Synthesis of amine-functionalized MnFe2O4@SiO2 magnetic nanoparticles with 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS)
Synthesis of graphene quantum dots using citric acid as carbon source
Loading the DAU drug on the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform
Assessment of apoptotic and necrotic cells by annexin V-FITC/PI staining
In vitro study of cell cycle
Investigation of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU nanoprobe based FRET system and calculation of detection limit

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Magnetization curves of MnFe2O4 (a), MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots (b).
SEM graphs of MnFe2O4 NPs (200 nm and 500 nm).
Element mapping for MnFe2O4 @SiO2 NPs and the EDX chart MnFe2O4 NPs (a), MnFe2O4@SiO2NPs (b).
FT-IR spectra of MnFe2O4@SiO2 (a), GQDs (b), MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots (c).
Absorption and emission spectrum of the graphene quantum dots.
UV–vis spectrum for MnFe2O4 NPs (a), MnFe2O4@SiO2 NPs (b) and MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots (c).
TEM images of MnFe2O4@SiO2 (a), MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots (b,c).
Emission spectra for graphene quantum dots (a), MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots (b), MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU (c).
Emission spectrum of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoprobe with enhancement DAU molar ratio (0 to 2).

Changes of fluorescence intensity at 460 nm after exposure to DAU. Inset: the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 460 nm and the concentration of DAU (R2 = 0.9952). Study of DAU loading and release by MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform.
DAU release from MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU at pH = 7.4 and pH = 5.5. Evaluation of therapeutic efficacy and cytotoxicity.

Percentage of cell viability for the HEK-293 normal cells and MCF-7 cancer cells in various concentrations (3.125–100 µg/mL) of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots (a), MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU nanobiosystem (b) and DAU (c) after 48 h incubation. The data represent means ± SEM after three experiments. Study of cell death mechanism with Annexin V-FITC/PI.
Effect of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU nanoplatform on the apoptotic amount of MCF-7 cancer cells. Study of the cell cycle in vitro.
Percentage of cell population at different stages of the cell cycle for (a) control sample (untreated cells), (b) MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU.
Schematic image of DAU loading on MnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots based MnFe2O4@SiO2 magnetic nanostructure as a pH-sensitive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system to enhance the anticancer effect of the drug

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 51666-14766, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 51666-14766, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among the various methods of targeted drug delivery, magnetic nanoparticles been considered for a long time due to local drug delivery, reduction of side effects, and controlled drug release. In this work, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU with 28.02 emu g-1 magnetism was prepared as pH-sensitive nanoplatform to enhance the anti-cancer effect of daunorubicin (DAU) drug (in the obtained FRET system, DAU act as acceptor molecule and graphene quantum dots act as donor molecule). The efficiency of the drug loaded on the nanoplatform in vitro is 78 %. DAU drug release from nanoplatform at pHs of 7.4 and 5.5 during 48 h is 21 % and 60 %, respectively. Release sensitive to pH facilitates the application of prepared nanoplatform for DAU delivery. The results of MTT-assay and annexin V-FITC/PI show that MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU induces cell apoptosis by inhibiting the growth of more than 95 % of MCF-7 cells. Also, according to the results, it was found that MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU can inhibit 66.65 % cell cycle in the sub-G1 phase. Therefore, due to the anti-cancer activity of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU, this biological nanoscale can be considered a candidate for drug delivery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional chemotherapy agents not only specifically affect cancer cells, but also damage other cells in the body. This means that the drug not only kills cancer cells, but also affects healthy cells and has a destructive effect on them. These are factors that reduce the therapeutic efficacy and increase the side effects of anticancer drugs. Therefore, the design and development of efficient drug delivery nanosystems with the ability to improve the efficiency of therapeutic agents is the basic need that new pharmaceutical sciences face (Tang et al., 2021). Recent developments in the drug targeted delivery process maintain the appropriate concentration of the drug over a long period time and reduce many of the common therapeutic limitations such as the number of doses used, initial drug concentration and side effects of simple drug release (Tewabe, 2021). Nanocarrier-based drug delivery systems have now entered the world pharmaceutical market, and their use in drug delivery is increasing day by day (Farani, 2020). Increasing the penetration of the drug in the target cells, increasing the solubility of the drug, reducing fluctuations, reducing the concentration of the drug in blood, and the possibility of targeting cancer tissues and cells with magnetic nanoparticles are the benefits of using nanoparticles in drug delivery systems (Yoo, 2019). The most important feature of magnetic nanoplatforms is the ability to control them outside the body by applying an external magnetic field, in such a way that the drug can be delivered to the exact location of the disease by applying a magnetic field from outside the body (Nguyen, 2021). Using biocompatible materials, smart nanomedicines can be made to treat cancer. By using this method, the speed, time and place of drug release can be controlled and the side effects of chemical drugs that are frequently used by patients can be minimized (Farani, 2020). Recently, graphene and its derivatives have been considered drug carriers due to their unique physico-chemical and optical properties such modifiable active groups, biocompatibility and thermal-optical effect. Graphene, graphene oxide (GO) and its reduced form, graphene quantum dots, and graphene composites been introduced as promising options to control the release of various drugs such as anticancer drugs, low-solubility drugs, antibiotics, antibodies, peptides,and genes (Campbell, 2019; McCallion, 2016). Graphene quantum dots have attracted a great deal of attention in the field of drug delivery due to their prominent properties physico-chemical, low toxicity, and oxygenated functional groups. Due to fluorescence properties, graphene quantum dots can act as an ideal nanoplatform with the function of detectable anticancer drug delivery and delivery to cancer cells without the intervention of fluorescent markers. Graphene quantum dots also can act as extinguishers and extinguish the fluorescent properties of compounds. This problem delays the development of targeted drug delivery systems with graphene quantum dots based traceability (Zhao, 2020). By precise design, the quenching of the fluorescent properties of compounds by graphene quantum dots can be used well. Therefore, the development of nanoplatforms based on graphene quantum dots with outstanding advantages can give rise to a new field in targeted drug delivery systems with traceability (Ding, 2017). Graphene quantum dots can be produced via top-down routes, including electrochemical oxidation, microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis, acid exfoliation, etc., or bottom-up methods including carbonization of certain organic precursors, solution chemistry,and fragmentation of suitable precursors such as C60 prepared (Sweetman, 2019).

This study is the first report on the synthesis of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU nanoprobe with the ability to deliver DAU anticancer drug and imaging simultaneously. The synthesized nanoprobe increases the anti-cancer effect of DAU by inhibiting 95 % growth of MCF-7 cells, inducing cell apoptosis and inhibiting the cell cycle in the sub-G1 stage.

Materials required for synthesis are as follows: manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate (Merck Co, Germany), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (Merck Co, Germany), tetraethyl orthosilicate (Merck Co, Germany), 3-Aminopropyl trimethoxysilane 97 % (Merck Co, Germany), absolute ethanol (Merck Co, Germany), sodium acetate anhydrous (Merck Co, Germany), glycerol (Merck Co, Germany), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride 98 % ≥98 % (Sigma–Aldrich Co, USA), N-Hydroxysuxxinimide 98٪ (Sigma–Aldrich Co, USA), dialysis bag 1000 MWCO (Sigma–Aldrich Co, USA), ammonium hydroxide solution 25 % (Merck Co, Germany), annexin V-labeled FITC apoptosis detection kit purchased from TAKARA (Tokyo, Japan), DAU were obtained from Exir Nano Sina Company (Tehran, Iran) and cell line MCF-7 from National Cell Bank of Iran, Pasteur Institute (Tehran, Iran).

Functional groups were identified by the technique of FTIR) AVATAR, Thermo company, USA), the amount of magnetism was measured by VSM (LBKFB, Kashan Desert Magnet Company, Iran), Powder X-ray diffraction pattern was recorded with step size of 0.05 deg and time Per Step of 1 s (PW1730, Philips Company, Netherlands), absorption and emission spectra were recorded by UV–Visible (Biomate5, Thermo. Company, USA) and PL spectrum (G9800A, Agilent Company). The morphology of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles were investigated by SEM (FESEM, MIRA III, TESCAN, Czech Republic) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TEM Philips EM 208S). The constituent elements of the sample were determined by EDX (FESEM, detector model MIRA II SAMX, TESCAN, Czech Republic).

MnFe2O4 nanoparticles with spinel structure were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Briefly, metal salts of FeCl3·6H2O (0.004 mol, 1080 mg) and MnCl2·4H2O (0.002 mol, 396 mg) in the molar ratio of Fe3+: Mn2+= 2:1, were dissolved in 50 ml of mixture of water and glycerol in a ratio of 1: 1 (Akhlaghi et al., 2020; Kurtinaitiene, 2016). After adding 1600 mg CH3COONa (0.019 mol) to the resulting mixture, the sample was stirred at room temperature for 0.5 h to homogenize. The resulting solution was transferred to an autoclave 50 ml and heated in an oven at 180° C for 12 h. After cooling the autoclave at room temperature, the precipitate formed was separated by a magnet. Magnetic MnFe2O4 nanoparticles were obtained after washing the precipitate with water and ethanol and dried at 60° C (Mirzaei-Kalar et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles coated with a silica layer
ce:section.ce:para: Several methods been developed including coating the surface of nanoparticles with gold, polymers, and silica to stabilize, prevent clumping and reduce the cytotoxicity of magnetic cores (Lyon et al., 2004). Among these, silica is the most widely used material for coating (Ngomsik et al., 2005), because it is a non-toxic, biocompatible, and chemically inert substance. For this purpose, to prevent the accumulation of nanoparticles and improve their chemical stability, a silica layer was formed around the MnFe2O4 nanoparticles. For this purpose, 132 mg (0.57 mmol) of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles were dispersed by sonicate in 50 ml of solvent (mixture of water and ethanol with a volume ratio of 4: 1). After adding 0.8 ml (3.6 mmol) TEOS and 2 ml NH3 aqueous solution, the resulting mixture was stirred for 15 h at 50° C under N2 gas. The obtained solid product was separated by a magnet and dried after washing with ethanol in a vacuum oven at 60° C (Kiani.ejad et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized MnFe2O4@SiO2 magnetic nanoparticles with 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS)
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of magnetic nanoparticles MnFe2O4@SiO2 with amine functional group was performed using the following method: 200 mg (0.86 mmol) of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles coated with silica layer were dispersed by sonicate vibrations for 10 min in 30 ml of absolute ethanol. In the next step, 0.6 ml (2.56 mmol) of APTMS was added to the mixture and the resulting suspension was refluxed for 24 h at 90° C under gas N2. The collected precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven after five times washing with ethanol (Kiani.ejad et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots using citric acid as carbon source
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare graphene quantum dots was used the citric acid precursor. A 5 ml glass containing 10.41 mmol of citric acid was placed in an oven at 200° C for 25 min. During the pyrolysis process, citric acid became an orange viscous liquid. The orange liquid was added to 100 ml of solution NaOH (0.025 M) under vigorous stirring. The pH of the sample was then adjusted to 7. To purify the resulting yellow solution, the sample was transferred to a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO) and dialyzed for 24 h. Finally, the solvent was removed by rotary and the yellow powder of graphene quantum dots was obtained (Dong, 2012).

The magnetic nanoplatform MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots was synthesized by this method: 500 mg graphene quantum dots precipitate was dissolved in 50 ml of saline buffer (PBS) (pH = 7.4). Then 800 mg of EDC coupling reagent and 100 mg of NHS were added to the sample. The resulting solution was stirred for 30 min at room temperature and in a dark environment. After adding 10 mg of MnFe2O4@SiO2-NH2 magnetic nanoparticles to the mixture, the sample was stirred at ambient temperature for 24 h. The final precipitate was separated by centrifugation and, after several times washing with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4), dried in a vacuum system (Su, 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading the DAU drug on the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform
ce:section.ce:para:
Briefly, 10 mg of DAU and 10 mg of magnetic nanoparticles MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots were mixed together in 20 ml of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). The resulting suspension was after stirring for 24 h at room temperature, centrifuge and the final product was dried in a vacuum. The concentration of free drug was determined by reading the adsorption of the supernatant solution at 485 nm (Xu, 2016).
(1)
L o a d i n g
C a p a c i t y
( % )
=
C i
-
C r
m
× 100

In this relation Ci, Cr and m are: the initial concentration of DAU drug, residual DAU concentration and nanoplatform mass, respectively.
(2)
L o a d i n g
E f f i c i e n c y
( % )
=
C i
-
C r
C i
× 100
In this equation, Ci represents the initial concentration of DAU and Cr represents the residual concentration of DAU.

MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the free drug, nanoplatform designed and nanoplatform containing drug on MCF-7 cancer cells. The MCF-7 cancer cells at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well were cultured into 96-well plates. The plate was incubated an overnight at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. After the end of the incubation time, the culture medium was removed. The MCF-7 cells after treatment with different concentrations of nanoplatform, free DAU, MnFe2O4@SiO2@graphene quantum dots @DAU were incubated for 48 h. Then, MTT reagent with a concentration of 5 mg/mL−1 dissolved in PBS buffer was added to each well and the plate was placed in a 37 °C incubator with 5 % CO2 for another 4 h. In the final step, the wells were emptied and 200 μl of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to each well, after 5 min of stirring, the absorbance at 570 nm was read by a microplate reader (Elx808, Biotek, USA) (Hamishehkar, 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of apoptotic and necrotic cells by annexin V-FITC/PI staining
ce:section.ce:para: Annexin V and propidium iodide (PI) staining were used to determine the extent of cell apoptosis induced by MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots @DAU. Cells were cultured at a density of 1 × 105 (cells per well) in 6-well plates. After removing the culture medium, drug carrier nanoplatform was added to MCF-7 cancer cells inside the wells. Cells were collected after 48 h of incubation and dispersed in 100 µl of binding buffer and 8 µl of annexin V. After incubation in the dark for 15 min, the cells were isolated by centrifugation and treated again at 100 µl of binding buffer and 8 µl of PI for 10 min in the dark environment. Finally, the percentage of cells that survived after treatment with the desired compound or entered the apoptotic and necrotic phases was determined by flowcytometry (Khiavi, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro study of cell cycle
ce:section.ce:para: To study the cell cycle after culturing 1 × 105 MCF-7 cells in 6-well plates and incubating the plate at 37 °C for 24 h, certain amount of drug carrying nanoplatform was added to the cells. After 48 h of incubation treated cells were washed with PBS buffer and stabilized in cold ethanol. To study the changes in different stages of the cell cycle, treated cells and control samples were stained with PI and examined by flowcytometry (Kafil, 2015).

The structure of MnFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles and MnFe2O4 nanoparticles coated with a silica layer was investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis. The sharp peaks appearing at 2θ = 18.3˚, 30.1˚, 35.45˚, 43.1˚, 53.55˚, 57.15˚, 62.7˚, 71.75˚, 74.15˚ are attributed to plates with miller indices (hkl) (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620), (533), respectively. The PXRD pattern of Fig. 1 corresponds to the standard PXRD pattern of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles (JCPDS Card No. 0034–75) and confirms the spinel structure of the synthesized nanoparticles. Also, the decrease of peaks intensity in the PXRD pattern forming the silica layer around the MnFe2O4 nanoparticles shows that the coating of the nanoparticles with the silica layer does not change the phase of the synthesized nanoparticles (Deraz and Alarifi, 2012).

The vibrating sample magnetometer technique was used to measure the magnetism of the samples. The magnetism of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles and MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform is equal to 49.08 emu g-1 and 28.02 emu g-1, respectively (Fig. 2 ). Reduction in magnetic property can be attributed to the formation of silica layer and the binding of graphene quantum dots to nanoparticles (Hassan, 2019).

The morphology and size of the nanoparticles was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The images of Fig. 3 shows nanoparticles are sphere shaped and have an average size of 39.84 nm. Also, the constituent elements of the MnFe2O4 nanoparticles and MnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were identified using X-ray energy diffraction (EDX) spectroscopy. The EDX diagram shows the MnFe2O4 nanoparticles are composed of the elements iron (Fe), manganese (Mn), and oxygen (O) (Fig. 4 a). The appearing of silica (Si) in the EDX diagram in Fig. 4b confirms the formation of silica layer around MnFe2O4 nanoparticles.

The FT-IR spectra of MnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots and MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform are shown in Fig. 5 . The bands appearing in 803 cm-1and 1082 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrations of the Si-OH and Si-O-Si bonds, respectively. Observation of these bands indicates the formation of layer silica around the nanoparticles (Fig. 5a). In the FT-IR spectrum of graphene quantum dots, the peaks of 1261 cm−1, 1562 cm−1 and 3418 cm−1 belong to the bending vibrations of the CH bond, the stretching vibrations of CO and the hydroxyl functional group, respectively. (Fig. 5b) (Ghanbari, 2021). Also, in the FT-IR spectrum of the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots, the 443 cm−1 and 562 cm−1 vibration bands are related to the Mn-O and Fe-O bonds. The 2918 cm−1 band belonging to CH aliphatic represents the amine functional group on the surface of MnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. The bands observed at 1289 cm−1 and 1623 cm−1 are attributed to CN bond vibrations and the amide bond formed between the MnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots (Fig. 5c).

The graphene quantum dots absorption wavelength was measured using UV–vis spectroscopy. In the UV–vis spectrum of graphene quantum dots, two absorption bands are observed (Fig. 6 a). The absorption bands 254 nm and 360 nm belong to the π-π* transitions of the CC bonds of the aromatic rings and the transitions n-π* of the CO group, respectively (Goswami, 2020; Reagen, 2021). The emission spectra of graphene quantum dots in the range of 600–400 and the excitation wavelength of 360 nm show the maximum propagation intensity at the wavelength of 460 nm (Fig. 6b). The absorption and emission properties of graphene quantum dots are related to the edge and surface functional groups (Pimsin, 2021).

To further study and ensure the formation of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots, the UV–vis spectra of MnFe2O4 nanoparticles, MnFe2O4 nanoparticles coated with silica and MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots were compared (Fig. 7 ). In the UV–vis spectrum MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4@SiO2 magnetic nanoparticles, no absorption bands are observed. Graphene quantum dots show a strong adsorption bands at 360 nm. (Fig. 6a). The characteristic peak observation of graphene quantum dots at 370 nm in the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots absorption spectrum confirms the formation of nanoplatform (Mirzaei-Kalar et al., 2022).

The morphology of MnFe2O4@SiO2 and the attachment of graphene quantum dots to MnFe2O4@SiO2 were investigated by TEM. The obtained TEM images confirm the attachment of graphene quantum dots to the MnFe2O4@SiO2 nanostructure (Fig. 8 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU nanoprobe based FRET system and calculation of detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: FRET is a special technique for measuring the distance between two chromophores known as an acceptor coupler. The limitation of FRET is that this process only works when the distance between acceptor and donor is less than 10 nm. However, FRET is a highly distance-dependent phenomenon and has become a popular tool for measuring the dynamic activities of biological molecules inside nanomaterials. The FRET mechanism involves a donor molecule in an excited electron state that can transfer its excitation energy to a nearby acceptor molecule non-radiatively. This energy transition is accompanied by reducing or eliminating the fluorescence intensity of the donor molecule and an increase in the fluorescence intensity of the acceptor molecule. For FRET to occur, the following conditions must be met: 1) the fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor molecule must overlap with the excitation or absorption spectrum of the receptor, 2) the donor and acceptor molecules must be very close (Usually about 1–10 nm), 3) the lifetime of the donor Fluorescent should be long enough to allow FRET to occur, 4) the direction of the momentum of the dipoles of the donor and acceptor molecules is important to ratio each other. MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU nanoprobe consist of the following components: 1) primary MnFe2O4 nanoparticles coated with silica, 2) graphene quantum dots: have fluorescent property and bind to MnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles with amine functional group by amide bonding. Finally, the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform by dual function can act as a fluorescent probe and donor compound in FRET, 3) DAU: with having fluorescent property and binding to graphene quantum dots by pi-pi interaction can act as an anticancer drug and receptor compound in FRET, thus having a dual function. The connection of these three components together leads to the formation of the FRET system, so that the adsorption of DAU on the surface of graphene quantum dots quenches the fluorescent property of graphene quantum dots (Su, 2017). After the nanoprobe is absorbed by the cancer cells and the DAU is released under the influence of the acidic environment inside the cancer cells, the fluorescent property of graphene quantum dots is activated (Scheme. 1 ).

The maximum emission intensity of graphene quantum dots is 460 nm and the anti-cancer drug DAU is excited at a wavelength of 485 nm. The overlap of the emission spectrum of graphene quantum dots with the DAU excitation wavelength creates a FRET system between the DAU and the graphene quantum dots. The emission spectra of graphene quantum dots, MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots and MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU can be observed in Fig. 9 . The fluorescent drug DAU binds to graphene quantum dots by pi-pi interaction. With loading DAU and the formation of the FRET system, the fluorescent property of graphene quantum dots is quenched and the emission spectrum of DAU appears at 573 nm. And vice versa, with the release of DAU, the intensity of the DAU emission spectrum decreases and the intensity of the graphene quantum dots emission spectrum increases (in other words, with the release of DAU, the emission of graphene quantum dots is activated).

To develop a FRET system, various nanoparticles are used, such as porous silica nanoparticles, quantum dots, gold nanoparticles, etc. A graphene quantum dots based FRET system aimed at drug delivery enables real-time monitoring of the drug release process through FRET signals (Chen, 2015). To study the FRET system, the concentration of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots was kept constant at 2 × 10-5 µM though the amounts of DAU changed from 0 to 2 × 10-5 µM (ri = 0, 0.8, 1.4, 1.6, 1.7, 1.9, 2). In the absence of DAU (ri = 0), the graphene quantum dots have the maximum emission intensity. By increasing the concentration of DAU in the environment, the intensity of the emission spectrum of graphene quantum dots decreases and the intensity of the emission spectrum of DAU increases. This trend indicates DAU uptake quenches the emission of graphene quantum dots and with the release of DAU, the emission of graphene quantum dots is activated (Fig. 10 ) (Mirzaei-Kalar et al., 2022).

The detection limits of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots were determined based on the emission spectrum data: LOD = 3 σ/S. Where LOD is the detection limit of the probe. σ is the criterion of the blank solution and S is regarded as the slope of the linear equation of the DAU fluorescence titration (Cheng, 2021). As shown in Fig. 11 , the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm gradually decreases with increasing DAU concentration. In a certain range of DAU concentration, there is a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 460 nm and DAU concentration (R2 = 0.9919). In experimental conditions, the calculated detection limit is 5.58 nm. Therefore, it can be concluded that the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoprobe is very sensitive toward DAU.

To measure DAU uptake by MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform, 10 mg DAU and 10 mg nanoparticles were dispersed in 20 ml PBS buffer with pH = 7.4. According to the absorption spectrum and the characteristic peak of DAU at 485 nm, the amount of drug adsorbed by the nanoplatform is 780 mg DAU per 1 gr of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots (780 mg/g) and the drug loading efficiency 78 %. The DAU release rate from the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU nanoprobe was investigated at pH = 7.4 (physiological conditions) and pH = 5.5 (acidic conditions) (Fig. 12 ). The results show that only 8.34 % of DAU is released after 5 h and 20.33 % of the drug after 24 h. In contrast, at pH = 5.5 (acidic conditions), 26.79 % and 58.75 % of the loaded DAU is released within 5 h and 24 h, respectively. After 2 days, about 60.61 % and 21.91 % of DAU drug is released at pH = 5.5 and pH = 7.4, respectively. Due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of graphene quantum dots with the amine and hydroxyl groups of DAU, the rate of DAU drug release under physiological conditions is low and can be said that the drug delivery system MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU is stable at pH = 7.4. The high rate of DAU release at pH = 5.5 can be explained by the protonation of the amine and carboxyl functional groups of DAU in acidic environment, which makes weakens the interaction between DAU and graphene quantum dots and consequently increases the solubility of DAU (Niu et al., 2013; Liu, 2007; Liu, 2009). Thus, the pH-dependent release of the drug from the designed nanoprobe and the acidity of the cancer cells environment indicate the suitability of the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots system as a substrate for delivery of the DAU anticancer drug (Gao, 2012; Ganta, 2008).

Toxicity of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatformt, MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU and DAU on HEK-293 normal cells and MCF-7 cancer cells at various concentrations (3.125–100 µg/mL) in vitro by using MTT was evaluated. The results obtained from MTT-assay show that the percentage of viability MCF-7 cancer cells decreases with DAU loading on MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots. So that at a concentration of 100 µg/ml after loading DAU on MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots, cell survival decreases from 91 % to 5 %. As can be seen in Fig. 13 , in HEK-293 normal cells, the cell viability decreases with increasing the concentration of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots and MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU from 3.125 to 100 µg /mL. At concentration of 100 µg /mL, with DAU loading on the nanoplatform, the amounts of cell viability reduce from 95 % to 41 %. Although, the designed drug delivery nanosystem is able to inhibit the growth of MCF-7 cancer cells by 95 %, on the other hand, it affects the growth of HEK-293 normal cells and reduces the survival rate of normal cells. Therefore, we are trying to reduce the damage to healthy cells by preparing a targeted drug delivery system, in addition to reducing side effects and controlled release.
Fig. 14 shows the MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots @DAU induce 55.5 % of early apoptosis and 9.48 % of late apoptosis in treated MCF-7 cells.

The effect of DAU loaded on MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots nanoplatform upon different stages of the cell cycle was investigated by flow cytometry. The results can be seenin Fig. 15 . The images show that the drug-carrying nanoplatform induces cell apoptosis by inhibiting the cell cycle before the G1 phase and increasing cell distribution in the sub-G1 phase.

Recently, various types of fluorescent materials such as semiconductor quantum dots, organic dyes (Luo, 2011; Li and Yao, 2009; Terai and Nagano, 2008) and lanthanide-doped nanocrystals (Zhang, 2014; Wei, 2014; Zhou et al., 2012) been considered. However, semiconductor quantum dots are limited by their toxicity and lanthanide-doped nanocrystals by their high cost. In addition, it should be noted that the platform with high loading capacity, such as graphene or GO, can lead to luminescence quenching when combined with fluorescent materials (Kim, 2010; Loh, 2010; Wei, 2012; Liu et al., 2011). Therefore, it is desirable to construct a multifunctional nanoplatform with intrinsic fluorescent properties that has the advantages of graphene or GO. Such drug delivery systems can perform drug delivery to the tumor, drug delivery monitoring, and imaging without the need for semiconductor quantum dots or external organic dyes. Fortunately, graphene quantum dots not only have the advantages of GO and graphene, but also have intrinsic fluorescence. However, so far, there is no report on the fabrication of a multifunctional drug delivery system based on MnFe2O4-graphene quantum dots. Hence, we prepared a multifunctional nanoplatform based on MnFe2O4@SiO2-NH2/graphene quantum dots that enables drug delivery monitoring by recording FRET signals. Also, the biological properties of MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU were investigated using MTT-assay, annexin V-FITC/PI, and cell cycle, and it was found that MnFe2O4@SiO2@ graphene quantum dots /DAU, by inhibiting the high growth of MCF-7 cells, induces apoptosis in cells and inhibits cell cycle in the sub-G1 phase. Therefore, it can be said that graphene quantum dots -based drug delivery systems, due to their fluorescent property, have a high potential for accurate tumor diagnosis, increasing drug efficacy, and cancer treatment with the drug traceability.

Zahra Kiani Nejad: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ali Akbar Khandar: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Massoumeh Khatamian: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
